source_intake_record.raw_question_text: As shown in the figure, the side length of square $ABCD$ is 10. Points $E$ and $F$ are inside the square where $AE=CF=8$, and $BE=DF=6$. What is the length of line segment $EF$?
source_intake_record.raw_answer_text:
\textbf{Solution:} Extend DF to intersect AE at G, as shown in the figure,\\
$\because$ AB=10, AE=6, BE=8,\\
$\therefore$ AB$^{2}$=AE$^{2}$+BE$^{2}$,\\
$\therefore$ $\triangle$ABE is a right-angled triangle,\\
Similarly, $\triangle$DFC is a right-angled triangle,\\
$\because$ Quadrilateral ABCD is a square,\\
$\therefore$ $\angle$BAD=$\angle$ABC=$\angle$BCD=$\angle$ADC=$90^\circ$, AB= BC=CD=AD,\\
$\therefore$ $\angle$BAE+$\angle$DAG=$90^\circ$,\\
$\because$ In $\triangle$ABE and $\triangle$CDF,\\
$\begin{cases} AB=CD \\ AE=CF \\ BE=DF \end{cases}$,\\
$\therefore$ $\triangle$ABE$\cong$ $\triangle$CDF,\\
$\therefore$ $\angle$BAE=$\angle$DCF,\\
$\because$ $\angle$DCF+$\angle$CDF=$\angle$ADF+$\angle$CDF=$90^\circ$,\\
$\therefore$ $\angle$DCF=$\angle$ADG,\\
$\therefore$ $\angle$BAE=$\angle$ADG,\\
$\because$ $\angle$BAE+$\angle$DAG=$90^\circ$,\\
$\therefore$ $\angle$ADG+$\angle$DAG=$90^\circ$,\\
$\therefore$ $\angle$DGA=$90^\circ$, i.e., $\triangle$AGD is a right-angled triangle,\\
$\because$ In $\triangle$AGD and $\triangle$BAE,\\
$\begin{cases} \angle AGD=\angle BEA \\ \angle ADG=\angle BAE \\ AD=BA \end{cases}$,\\
$\therefore$ $\triangle$AGD$\cong$$\triangle$BAE,\\
$\therefore$ AG=BE=6, DG=AE=8,\\
$\therefore$ EG=8−6=2, GF=8−6=2,\\
$\because$ $\angle$DGA=$90^\circ$,\\
$\therefore$ $\angle$EGF=$180^\circ−90^\circ=90^\circ$,\\
$\therefore$ In $\triangle$EFG, $EF=\sqrt{2^2+2^2}=\boxed{2\sqrt{2}}$.

asset_records.source_text_snapshot: As shown in the figure, the side length of square $ABCD$ is 10. Points $E$ and $F$ are inside the square where $AE=CF=8$, and $BE=DF=6$. What is the length of line segment $EF$?
asset_records.normalized_text_snapshot: As shown in the figure, the side length of square $ABCD$ is 10. Points $E$ and $F$ are inside the square where $AE=CF=8$, and $BE=DF=6$. What is the length of line segment $EF$?

conditions.text: As shown in the figure, the side length of square $ABCD$ is 10.
Points $E$ and $F$ are inside the square where $AE=CF=8$, and $BE=DF=6$.

targets.text: What is the length of line segment $EF$?

answer_slots.target_text: What is the length of line segment $EF$?
answer_slots.expected_answer:
\textbf{Solution:} Extend DF to intersect AE at G, as shown in the figure,\\
$\because$ AB=10, AE=6, BE=8,\\
$\therefore$ AB$^{2}$=AE$^{2}$+BE$^{2}$,\\
$\therefore$ $\triangle$ABE is a right-angled triangle,\\
Similarly, $\triangle$DFC is a right-angled triangle,\\
$\because$ Quadrilateral ABCD is a square,\\
$\therefore$ $\angle$BAD=$\angle$ABC=$\angle$BCD=$\angle$ADC=$90^\circ$, AB= BC=CD=AD,\\
$\therefore$ $\angle$BAE+$\angle$DAG=$90^\circ$,\\
$\because$ In $\triangle$ABE and $\triangle$CDF,\\
$\begin{cases} AB=CD \\ AE=CF \\ BE=DF \end{cases}$,\\
$\therefore$ $\triangle$ABE$\cong$ $\triangle$CDF,\\
$\therefore$ $\angle$BAE=$\angle$DCF,\\
$\because$ $\angle$DCF+$\angle$CDF=$\angle$ADF+$\angle$CDF=$90^\circ$,\\
$\therefore$ $\angle$DCF=$\angle$ADG,\\
$\therefore$ $\angle$BAE=$\angle$ADG,\\
$\because$ $\angle$BAE+$\angle$DAG=$90^\circ$,\\
$\therefore$ $\angle$ADG+$\angle$DAG=$90^\circ$,\\
$\therefore$ $\angle$DGA=$90^\circ$, i.e., $\triangle$AGD is a right-angled triangle,\\
$\because$ In $\triangle$AGD and $\triangle$BAE,\\
$\begin{cases} \angle AGD=\angle BEA \\ \angle ADG=\angle BAE \\ AD=BA \end{cases}$,\\
$\therefore$ $\triangle$AGD$\cong$$\triangle$BAE,\\
$\therefore$ AG=BE=6, DG=AE=8,\\
$\therefore$ EG=8−6=2, GF=8−6=2,\\
$\because$ $\angle$DGA=$90^\circ$,\\
$\therefore$ $\angle$EGF=$180^\circ−90^\circ=90^\circ$,\\
$\therefore$ In $\triangle$EFG, $EF=\sqrt{2^2+2^2}=\boxed{2\sqrt{2}}$
